lesson: L26 — Right-Triangle Congruence: HL, LL, HA, LA
banner: Week 30 · Prove It — HL, LL, HA, LA
essential_question: Can you write a COMPLETE two-column proof using HL, LL, HA, or LA — not just recognize the shortcut when it's already labeled?
objective: Write a two-column proof (statements and reasons) that two right triangles are congruent using HL, LL, HA, or LA, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch two right triangles and write, from memory, the four names HL, LL, HA, LA and the two parts each one requires. Then check against your concept note.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 26A & 26B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 26A/26B. We check the tricky ones: the isosceles-triangle proof that used HL to reach 'C is the midpoint of BD' (is BC ≅ DC alone enough, or do you need one more step?), the equilateral-triangle proof that used LA, and the two circle-tangent proofs on 26B built on LL and LA. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: HL is the one safe exception to 'Side-Side-Angle doesn't prove congruence.' Argue it — with the hypotenuse and one leg fixed, the Pythagorean theorem gives the third leg exactly ONE possible value, so there's no ambiguous second triangle to worry about.

Claim 2: given ∠PQS and ∠RQS are right angles and PQ ≅ RQ, argue out loud why ΔPQS ≅ ΔRQS by LL — name QS as the shared side and explain why the Reflexive Property lets a single shared leg count as 'two legs given.'

Claim 3: LA can mean either ASA or AAS depending on where the given leg sits. Argue why BOTH arrangements still work — in each case you end up with two angles (the right angle plus the given angle) and one side, which is always enough to fix a triangle.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 27: similar triangles and AA similarity — where 'same shape' replaces 'same size.'

success_criteria.text: I can choose the correct right-triangle shortcut (HL, LL, HA, or LA) given which parts are marked congruent
I can explain why HL is valid despite looking like Side-Side-Angle, and connect each shortcut to its general postulate (SSS, SAS, ASA, or AAS)
I can write a complete two-column proof (statements and reasons) that two right triangles are congruent using HL, LL, HA, or LA

questions.prompt: Two right triangles each have BOTH legs marked congruent (no hypotenuse or angle marked). Which shortcut applies?
questions.explain: Two matching legs, with the right angle already free between them, is the LL (Leg-Leg) postulate.

questions.prompt: HA is really which general triangle congruence postulate in disguise?
questions.explain: The right angle and the given acute angle are the two angles; the hypotenuse (opposite the right angle) is not between them, so HA is AAS.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why HL is the one safe exception to 'Side-Side-Angle does not prove triangles congruent.'
questions.answer: SSA usually fails because two sides and a non-included angle can close into two different triangles (the ambiguous case). But when that angle is a right angle, the Pythagorean theorem forces the third side to exactly one value — c² − a² = b² has only one positive solution — so there's no second triangle possible. That single-value guarantee is what makes HL safe.
questions.explain: The right angle removes the ambiguity that makes SSA unreliable everywhere else.

questions.prompt: Complete the proof. Given: ∠PQS and ∠RQS are right angles; PQ ≅ RQ. Prove: ΔPQS ≅ ΔRQS. Statements: (1) ∠PQS and ∠RQS are right angles. (2) PQ ≅ RQ. (3) QS ≅ QS. (4) ΔPQS and ΔRQS are right triangles. (5) ΔPQS ≅ ΔRQS. Fill in the REASON for each of the five statements above.
questions.answer: (1) Given. (2) Given. (3) Reflexive Property. (4) Definition of a right triangle (each triangle contains a right angle). (5) LL Postulate — leg QS (shared) and leg PQ ≅ RQ (given) are two legs of one right triangle congruent to two legs of the other.
questions.explain: QS is the side the two triangles share, so it is congruent to itself by the Reflexive Property — that shared leg plus the given leg PQ ≅ RQ is exactly the LL postulate.

questions.prompt: A carpenter has two right-triangle braces. Both have a 7-inch leg adjacent to a 35° angle. Which shortcut proves the braces congruent, and why doesn't the carpenter need to check the hypotenuse at all?
questions.answer: LA (Leg-Angle) — the leg and the adjacent angle already force the rest of the triangle (this is ASA, since the leg sits between the right angle and the 35° angle). The hypotenuse's length is fixed by trig once the leg and angle are set, so measuring it would only confirm what's already guaranteed.
questions.explain: Once a leg and its adjacent acute angle match, every other part of the triangle — including the hypotenuse — is determined; there's nothing left to check.

questions.prompt: Which of the four right-triangle shortcuts is the odd one out, because it relies on the Pythagorean theorem to pin down a missing side rather than directly matching an included angle or included side?
questions.explain: LL, HA, and LA all map directly onto SAS, AAS, or ASA using parts you're simply given. HL is the exception — the third side isn't given, it's forced by the Pythagorean theorem.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 26 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one shortcut — HL, LL, HA, or LA — you'd want to drill more before moving on to similar triangles next week.
